title: CrossModal Study
description: The CrossModal Study investigates how the brain prepares for and discriminates targets across sensory modalities using concurrent frequency-tagged visual and auditory stimulation. Participants performed a target-discrimination task with auditory or visual cues followed by a 3-second preparation interval during which a 36 Hz frequency-tagged fixation cross and 40 Hz amplitude-modulated tone were presented. Targets were either visual Gabor patches or auditory tones, presented with or without cross-modal distractors. The dataset includes MEG recordings, behavioral responses, and eye-tracking data from healthy control participants.
methods_description: MEG data were acquired during a cued target-discrimination task with two experimental blocks. Block 1 contained unimodal trials (auditory cue-target or visual cue-target). Block 2 contained cross-modal trials with targets presented alongside distractors from the opposite modality. A 3-second preparation interval between cue and target featured frequency-tagged visual stimulation (36 Hz) and amplitude-modulated auditory stimulation (40 Hz). Task difficulty was individually calibrated prior to recording. Eye-tracking data were collected to monitor eye movements. Participants responded using a button box to identify one of three targets per modality.
